content.text: What it is

Eval-linked success criteria connect each acceptance criterion in the product requirements to a specific, automated evaluation test case. Rather than keeping success criteria in a product specification document and evaluation tests in a separate code repository with no formal linkage between them, eval-linked success criteria treat the two as a single artifact: every criterion has an ID, and every test case references that ID. This linkage ensures that every requirement is tested and every test traces back to a stated requirement. When a test fails, the team knows exactly which acceptance criterion was violated. When an acceptance criterion is changed, the team knows exactly which tests must be updated.
Why it matters

AI products fail in ways that are subtle and hard to detect: a system may handle 95% of inputs correctly while failing systematically on a specific class of inputs that matters most to the business. Without evaluation cases explicitly mapped to acceptance criteria, these failures go undetected until they surface in production. Eval-linked success criteria make the coverage visible: you can see exactly which acceptance criteria are covered by automated tests and which are not. This visibility is essential for managing the risk of shipping AI features to production, and it is often required by legal and compliance teams in regulated industries who need evidence that specific behavioral requirements were verified before launch.
Benefits
Complete traceability: every requirement is covered by at least one test, and every test failure points to a specific requirement — eliminating ambiguity in failure triage.
Regression prevention: the CI gate blocks merges that break any acceptance criterion, catching regressions before they reach users.
Change impact analysis: when an acceptance criterion changes, the linked test IDs make it immediately clear which tests need to be updated or added.
Compliance evidence: the criterion-to-test linkage provides auditors with proof that each stated requirement was verified by an automated test before production deployment.
Quality bar enforcement: tiered quality bars (alpha, beta, GA) with linked eval thresholds create objective criteria for promotion between deployment stages.
Trade-offs and limitations
Overhead of maintaining linkage: as requirements change, the mapping between criteria IDs and test IDs must be kept current, which requires discipline and tooling.
Test coverage illusion: having a test for every criterion does not guarantee the tests are good tests — a weak test for an important criterion gives false confidence.
LLM-as-judge costs: behavioral criteria that require semantic grading (not exact match) require inference calls in the evaluation suite, adding latency and cost to CI.
Brittleness at spec boundaries: criteria defined too narrowly create test suites that pass on the letter of the specification while missing the spirit of what the feature should do.
How it works

The linkage is established at specification time. Each acceptance criterion (AC) gets a unique identifier (AC-001, AC-002, etc.) and is written in Given/When/Then format with a measurable pass condition. Each evaluation test case (EVAL-001, EVAL-002, etc.) includes a maps_to field listing the AC IDs it validates. The evaluation runner outputs a coverage matrix: rows are ACs, columns are EVAL cases, and cells indicate pass/fail. This matrix is the primary artifact for CI gate decisions and compliance reviews.

Quality bars define the minimum pass rate required for each deployment stage. A common tiering is: alpha requires 70% with manual review, beta requires 85% with automated CI gate and monitored rollout, and general availability requires 92% with automated CI gate, production drift monitoring, and security/legal review. These thresholds are explicitly stored in the specification file so that the CI runner can enforce them without requiring human interpretation.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Four-stage pipeline from success criteria to eval suite to CI gate to production, with quality bar tiers below
attrs.title: Eval-Linked Success Criteria Pipeline

content.text: Success criteria define the acceptance bars. The eval suite maps each test to a criterion ID. The CI gate runs the suite on every PR and blocks merges that drop below the quality bar. Production monitoring continuously re-runs a sample of eval cases against live traffic to detect behavioral drift.
When to use it

Apply eval-linked success criteria for any AI feature where quality must be demonstrated objectively — which in practice means any feature that serves real users. Internal tools and PoC experiments can use lighter-weight approaches, but any feature targeting external users, regulated workflows, or business-critical processes should have formal eval linkage in place before the first production deployment.
How to implement

Write acceptance criteria with unique IDs and explicit pass conditions. Each AC should describe the input scenario, the expected behavior, and the minimum acceptable performance (e.g., 'in 92% or more of test cases').

Create test cases that map to criteria IDs. Store each test case as a JSON file with: id, maps_to (list of AC IDs), input, expected_behavior, and scoring_method (exact_match, llm_judge, or rule_based).
Build the evaluation runner that loads criteria thresholds from the spec, runs all test cases, computes pass rates per AC, and outputs a coverage matrix with a pass/fail decision for each AC.
Integrate the runner into CI as a required check on all PRs that touch the system prompt, few-shot examples, or specification. Set the deployment stage's quality bar as the merge threshold.
Run the same eval suite in production on a daily sample of real traffic, using the same criterion IDs to detect behavioral drift after model updates or data distribution changes.
Examples
Acceptance criteria specification with eval linkage metadata:

# feature: invoice-extraction-agent
# deployment_stage: general_availability

quality_bars:
  alpha:    { min_pass_rate: 0.70, gate: "manual" }
  beta:     { min_pass_rate: 0.85, gate: "ci" }
  ga:       { min_pass_rate: 0.92, gate: "ci+prod_monitoring" }

current_stage: ga
current_threshold: 0.92

acceptance_criteria:
  - id: AC-001
    title: "Extract invoice number accurately"
    given: "A scanned invoice PDF is uploaded"
    when: "The extraction agent processes it"
    then: "The invoice number field is correctly extracted"
    pass_condition: "Exact match with ground truth in ≥ 95% of test cases"
    priority: critical
    eval_cases: [EVAL-001, EVAL-002, EVAL-003]

  - id: AC-002
    title: "Handle missing invoice number gracefully"
    given: "A PDF with no visible invoice number"
    when: "The extraction agent processes it"
    then: "Returns null with a confidence: low signal, does not hallucinate"
    pass_condition: "No hallucinated invoice number in ≥ 99% of such cases"
    priority: critical
    eval_cases: [EVAL-004, EVAL-005]

  - id: AC-003
    title: "Multi-vendor invoice format support"
    given: "Invoices from any of 15 known vendor templates"
    when: "The extraction agent processes them"
    then: "All required fields are extracted across all vendor formats"
    pass_condition: "Field extraction accuracy ≥ 90% across all vendor test cases"
    priority: high
    eval_cases: [EVAL-006, EVAL-007, EVAL-008, EVAL-009]

  - id: AC-004
    title: "Latency within processing SLA"
    given: "A PDF ≤ 10 pages"
    when: "The extraction agent processes it"
    then: "Response is returned within 8 seconds end-to-end"
    pass_condition: "p95 latency ≤ 8000ms across benchmark documents"
    priority: high
    eval_cases: [EVAL-010]
An evaluation runner that enforces the quality bar per acceptance criterion:
Real-world example

A healthcare AI company built a clinical documentation assistant. Without formal eval linkage, testing was a mix of manual spot-checks and a few automated scripts that were not connected to the requirements document. When a model upgrade was applied, 14 tests in one script started failing — but the team did not know which, if any, of the requirements these tests covered. The upgrade was shipped anyway because the developers believed the failing tests were 'edge cases.' Three weeks later, the system started producing documentation with incorrect medication dosage fields in a specific set of template forms — exactly the failure mode covered by the failing tests.

After the incident, the team adopted eval-linked success criteria. They wrote 62 acceptance criteria, each covering a specific documentation scenario, and mapped 187 test cases to them. The coverage matrix immediately revealed that one critical requirement — 'must not modify numeric dosage fields not present in source dictation' — had zero test coverage. Adding three targeted test cases for that requirement would have caught the regression before the model upgrade shipped. The evaluation runner was integrated into CI within a week, and the team has not shipped a behavioral regression since.
Common mistakes

Writing acceptance criteria without pass conditions — 'the AI should handle ambiguous inputs gracefully' is not testable; 'the AI should ask one clarifying question for ambiguous inputs in ≥ 90% of test cases' is.
Maintaining the criteria document and test files in separate repositories without tooling to enforce linkage integrity, which allows the two to drift out of sync.
Setting a single global quality bar (e.g., 92%) without per-criterion thresholds, which allows the average to mask a critical criterion with a 60% pass rate.
Using only happy-path test cases in the eval suite, which produces high pass rates while leaving adversarial and edge-case acceptance criteria uncovered.
Not running the eval suite in production on a sample of live traffic, so model behavioral drift goes undetected until it causes a visible user-facing failure.
Best practices

Write acceptance criteria before writing test cases — the criterion defines what must be true, and the test case verifies it. Reversing this order leads to tests that validate the implementation rather than the requirement.
Require at least two test cases per critical acceptance criterion to reduce the impact of any single poorly-written test case on the pass rate for that criterion.
Include adversarial test cases (designed to fail) in the eval suite with expected_behavior set to the refusal or safe-handling behavior, not just happy-path cases.
Generate the coverage matrix as a CI artifact so that reviewers can inspect per-criterion pass rates, not just the aggregate gate decision.
Set per-criterion thresholds for critical safety and compliance requirements that are higher than the overall quality bar — a safety criterion at 99% even when the overall bar is 92%.
Performance and scalability

The main performance concern for eval-linked success criteria is CI runtime. As the test suite grows, inference costs and wall-clock time for the CI gate increase. Common mitigation strategies: run the full suite nightly and a targeted subset (covering only the criteria affected by the PR's changes) on PRs; use a fast, cheap model (e.g., claude-haiku-4-5) for LLM-as-judge grading; cache system prompt tokens using prompt caching to reduce grading latency by 40–60%; and parallelize test case execution across multiple processes.

Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Traceability
Regression safety
Eval-linked criteria (this topic)
Full — each test maps to a criterion
High — CI blocks any criterion regression
Manual QA only
Low — informal, undocumented
Low — depends on reviewer attention
Automated tests without AC linkage
Medium — test coverage visible, but no requirement mapping
Medium — catches regressions but does not surface which requirement
Security and reliability

Security-critical acceptance criteria (prompt injection defense, PII handling, content filtering) should have their own dedicated quality bars set above the overall threshold — typically 99% or higher. These criteria should never be bundled into an overall pass rate calculation where they could be masked by high scores on other criteria. The coverage matrix must show per-criterion scores for all security-critical ACs explicitly so that reviewers cannot approve a deployment without seeing each one pass.
Key takeaways
Eval-linked success criteria create a traceable chain from requirement to test to CI gate — every requirement is covered and every failure is attributed.
Quality bars must be tiered by deployment stage (alpha, beta, GA) with explicit thresholds stored in the specification file so they are enforceable without human interpretation.
Coverage matrix visibility is as important as the overall pass rate — a critical criterion with 60% pass rate hidden inside a 93% overall average is a production risk.
Security and safety acceptance criteria should have higher thresholds than the overall quality bar and should be reported separately in the CI output.
Run the eval suite in production daily on a sample of live traffic to detect behavioral drift that only appears under real-world input distributions.
Scale the CI suite by running a full suite nightly and a targeted PR subset based on which criteria are affected by the change.
